ut2-3-1.title: Hands-on: catch the flaws lying along the grain
ut2-3-1.intro: Forging flow lays stringers, flakes, and bursts parallel to the grain, so you fire a 45° shear beam across the flow to catch them. Slide the wedge along the forged section and Mark each hidden reflector right at its echo peak.

tasks.label: Mark the near-surface stringer 8 mm down
tasks.hint: Slide the wedge slowly and press Mark within ±4 mm of the echo peak.

tasks.label: Mark the centerline burst at 15 mm depth
tasks.hint: Peak amplitude means the 45° beam centreline is square on the flaw — bursts live at mid-wall.

tasks.label: Mark the deep flake cluster at 22 mm
tasks.hint: Deeper reflector, longer sound path — its echo arrives later on the A-scan.

ut2-3-2.title: Hands-on: shrink λ toward grain size — then back off
ut2-3-2.intro: Grain scatter climbs roughly with f⁴, which is why castings are tested slow. Raise the frequency until λ in steel drops below 1 mm — coarse-grain territory where grass buries the screen — then see how aluminum behaves at the same settings.

tasks.label: Push λ below 1 mm in steel — grain-scatter range
tasks.hint: λ = v/f: at 5,920 m/s you need roughly 6 MHz or more.

tasks.label: Send the same pulse through steel and aluminum
tasks.hint: Switch materials at a fixed frequency and watch λ and the near field shift.

ut2-3-3.title: Hands-on: read echo depth at composite velocity
ut2-3-3.intro: A delamination reflects nearly everything, so it reads like an early back wall at the separation depth. Gauge a stepped CFRP block at ~3,000 m/s — half steel's velocity — and pick out the thinnest step by its earliest echo.

tasks.label: Record readings on three laminate steps
tasks.hint: Center the 0° probe on a step and log the back-wall reading before moving on.

tasks.label: Flag the thinnest step — the earliest echo
tasks.hint: Thinner section, shorter time of flight: the echo sits closest to the initial pulse.

ut2-3-4.title: Hands-on: velocity sets the rules — steel to water
ut2-3-4.intro: Rubber carries sound at water speed — about a quarter of steel — and that one number resets the wavelength, the time base, and every readout. Fire the same pulse through water, aluminum, and steel and watch the picture change.

tasks.label: Run the pulse in water, aluminum, and steel
tasks.hint: Water stands in for rubber at ~1,480 m/s — watch the wavefronts bunch up versus steel.

tasks.label: In steel, drive λ under 1 mm; then recheck water
tasks.hint: Same frequency, a quarter the velocity: λ = v/f, so water's λ is ~4× shorter.
